summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' answers reader questions on topics ranging from Pi Day date formats to grading anxiety to Seattle's minimum wage debate. The column opens with a meta-plea for readers to submit more questions (especially provocative ones) to keep the column alive. The advice given is characteristically irreverent: suggesting arbitrary mathematical bases could make every day 'Super Pi Day,' recommending teachers lower expectations and grade on a curve to preserve sanity, and noting that restaurant closures blamed on minimum wage hikes are likely statistical noise. The column closes with brief political commentary on Bernie Sanders' candidacy and how Obama's micro-targeting machine may have closed the door on future insurgent candidates.
key_points: Pi Day's 'once in a century' framing is arbitrary - using different date formats or number bases, almost every day could qualify as special
Teachers should cope with disappointing grades by lowering expectations beforehand and grading on a curve, treating any comprehension as a victory
Claims that Seattle restaurant closures are caused by minimum wage hikes should be viewed skeptically given the industry's inherent volatility
Obama built the modern political micro-targeting machine but 'closed the door behind him,' potentially blocking future insurgent candidates
The column maintains a deliberately absurdist, self-deprecating tone while sneaking in substantive points about statistics and political economy
money_quotes: Instead of being disappointed when not everyone understands everything, you have to be overjoyed when someone understands something.
I lower my expectations to the point where I'm like, man I hope someone passed this test.
That way my fantasies always come true, even if the scores are crowded down around 17.
It doesn't mean those days becomes less super, just that almost every day is super.
Anybody looking for evidence that they are going out of business for a given reason would have plenty of statistical noise ready and willing to distract them.
Obama was an insurgent and won but also built the modern micro-targeting machine. He closed the door behind him.
key_insight: Behind the column's irreverent humor lies a consistent skepticism toward arbitrary framing and cherry-picked evidence, whether applied to mathematical holidays, student assessment, or economic policy claims.
